script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Wednesday — here is your QQQM recap. The QQQ portfolio closed zero point seven six percent down on the session. It was a session defined by sharp divergence across the technology landscape — winners and losers separated by double digits, and one corner of the semiconductor space taking a meaningful hit that warrants a close look.

The headline story this Wednesday is the semiconductor selloff, and specifically what Samsung's quarterly earnings did to the broader memory chip sector. Despite Samsung reporting a profit surge of nineteen-fold — the most profitable quarter in the memory industry's history by some accounts — the market sold the news hard. Micron Technology fell three point eighty-one percent on the day. The disconnect is stark: record profits, falling stocks.

The broad market closed mixed on Wednesday, with the Nasdaq leading losses in the prior session and futures attempting a recovery heading into the open. Technology was the primary pressure point, with semiconductor names bearing the brunt of a sector-wide reassessment following Samsung's earnings release. No specific Federal Reserve data or major economic print was the primary driver; instead, geopolitical developments — including reported United States military strikes against Iran following attacks on commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz — added an undercurrent of uncertainty that kept risk appetite in check across the session.

The top story of Wednesday's session belongs to the memory chip sector, and it centers on Micron Technology. Samsung Electronics reported preliminary quarterly profit that surged nineteen-fold year-over-year — a figure that, under normal circumstances, would lift the entire sector. Instead, it had the opposite effect. Investors appear to have interpreted the Samsung result not as validation of demand, but as a signal that the elevated expectations baked into memory chip valuations may be running ahead of what the market can sustain. Micron fell three point eighty-one percent, closing at nine hundred thirty-eight dollars and thirty-eight cents — a decline of thirty-seven dollars and eighteen cents on the session. Meanwhile, reporting from finance.yahoo.com framed the situation directly: the most profitable quarter in the memory industry's history just knocked the sector's stocks lower. Investor Ross Gerber pushed back, calling the chip selloff, in his words, "a gift" for long-term investors, and describing semiconductor companies as among the least expensive in the market. Gerber's comments, shared on the platform X, were aimed at what he characterized as very short-term thinking among sellers. The broader pressure also tracked into global markets, with Japan's Nikkei declining in volatile trade as technology stocks faced overnight pressure from Nasdaq losses. For QQQM holders, Micron sits at five point seven five percent of the portfolio — the fourth largest weight — making Wednesday's move a meaningful drag on overall performance.

Turning now to the full holdings rundown, beginning with the portfolio's largest position. Nvidia closed at one hundred ninety-six dollars and ninety-three cents, up one point zero eight percent on the session — a gain of two dollars and ten cents. Nvidia held relatively well against the broader semiconductor pressure, and its resilience is notable given the sector headwinds. News attributed to Nvidia's ticker in Wednesday's session was largely off-topic — articles covering SpaceX valuation and unrelated corporate activity — meaning there was no Nvidia-specific catalyst driving the move. The stock's outperformance relative to other chip names suggests the market continues to treat Nvidia as a separate, higher-conviction AI infrastructure play rather than a pure memory-cycle trade.

Over at Apple, the stock closed at three hundred ten dollars and sixty-six cents, up zero point six six percent — a gain of two dollars and three cents. Two developments are worth noting. First, analyst Ming-Chi Kuo weighed in on the long-anticipated foldable iPhone, indicating it may not reach store shelves until the fourth quarter of twenty twenty-six — months after the broader iPhone eighteen lineup launches. Supply constraints are cited as the primary obstacle, with limited preorder availability expected even when the product does arrive. Second, and with more immediate relevance to the supply chain, Broadcom extended its chip supply agreement with Apple through twenty thirty-one, according to a regulatory filing reported by Bloomberg. That deal has long-standing roots and reinforces Apple's multi-year procurement strategy across its hardware lineup.

Alphabet closed at three hundred sixty-seven dollars and three cents, up one point nine eight percent — a gain of seven dollars and twelve cents. Alphabet was the strongest performer among the top three positions on Wednesday. The session's news included a developing story around FIFA World Cup broadcasting rights, with Alphabet's YouTube joining Netflix and Disney as reported contenders in what is shaping up to be a two-billion-dollar bidding war for United States rights to the twenty thirty and twenty thirty-four men's World Cups. FIFA is expected to begin formal talks with media companies in the coming months. A successful bid would extend YouTube's live sports footprint considerably and carries strategic weight for Alphabet's advertising and subscription revenue.

As covered in the top story, Micron Technology closed at nine hundred thirty-eight dollars and thirty-eight cents, down three point eighty-one percent, shedding thirty-seven dollars and eighteen cents. The Samsung earnings dynamic continues to be the central force here, and the position remains one to monitor closely given its portfolio weight of five point seven five percent.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-eight dollars and eighty-four cents, down zero point four two percent — a loss of one dollar and sixty-five cents. No material Microsoft-specific news emerged in Wednesday's session. The modest decline tracks with broader technology softness rather than any company-specific catalyst. Microsoft remains a key enterprise and cloud infrastructure holding within the portfolio.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-five dollars and ninety-eight cents, up one point three six percent, adding three dollars and thirty-one cents. Amazon outperformed the broader index in Wednesday's session with no specific headline catalyst — the move likely reflects ongoing strength in e-commerce and cloud sentiment rather than any single news item. Watch for any incremental commentary around AWS demand trends as we move through earnings season.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred sixteen dollars and eleven cents, down zero point three three percent — a loss of one dollar and seventy-one cents. The decline was modest, and in the context of the session's broader semiconductor pressure, Advanced Micro Devices held its ground reasonably well. No company-specific news drove the move.

Tesla closed at four hundred two dollars and ninety cents, up two point four zero percent — a gain of nine dollars and forty-five cents. Tesla was among the stronger performers in the portfolio on Wednesday, with no specific headline news to explain the move. The stock has been reactive to broad risk sentiment, and Wednesday's gain may reflect some rotation into growth and consumer discretionary names as semiconductor names absorbed selling pressure.

Meanwhile, Intel closed at one hundred ten dollars and thirty-nine cents, down eight point two eight percent — a loss of nine dollars and ninety-six cents. That is the steepest percentage decline among all primary holdings on Wednesday. Intel's move comes in a session where the broader semiconductor sector was already under pressure, and the magnitude of the loss stands out. No single headline exclusively drove the Intel decline, but sector-wide caution around AI chip demand durability and competitive positioning continue to weigh on the stock. At two point nine percent of the portfolio, the impact is contained — but the eight-plus percent single-session move is a number worth tracking.

Rounding out the secondary tier with two notable standouts. Meta Platforms closed at six hundred fifteen dollars and fifty-eight cents, up five point six one percent — a gain of thirty-two dollars and sixty-eight cents. That is a substantial single-session move. Applied Materials closed at five hundred fifty-four dollars and fifty cents, down eight point zero five percent — a loss of forty-eight dollars and fifty-four cents, in a session where semiconductor equipment names bore significant pressure alongside their chip-making counterparts.

Three items to track in the next forty-eight hours. First, the Federal Reserve's meeting minutes are due for release, referenced in Wednesday's futures coverage — any language around the pace of rate normalization will directly affect technology valuations across the portfolio. Second, SK Hynix's anticipated twenty-nine-billion-dollar United States stock market debut is expected later this week — as a direct Micron competitor, the listing and its reception will set tone for the entire memory chip segment. Third, watch the FIFA World Cup broadcasting rights process for any formal announcement involving Alphabet's YouTube — a confirmed bid or award would be a material catalyst for the communication services segment of this portfolio.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the Federal Reserve minutes closely — any shift in tone on rates could reset the entire technology trade heading into the back half of the week.
